figures.caption: Figure 3: Raw Top-K overlap (hit ratio) between consecutive decoding steps across different layers in DeepSeek-V3.2, measured on entry 1 of swe bench 64k.jsonl from SWE-bench-derived LongSeqTasks (2,025 generated tokens; 2,024 valid previous-step comparisons after skipping step 0). Here “raw” means that the current-step Top-K index set is compared directly against the previous-step Top-K index set without any positional shift. For readability, each curve is rendered with a 25-step moving average over decode steps, and the inset zooms into the 0–4% range for Layers 0 and 1. Layers 20–60 exhibit 35–50% average overlap (max ∼60%), while Layers 0 and 1 show near-zero overlap (∼1–2%), reflecting distinct indexer score dynamics in early vs. deeper layers.

Figure 6: Phase 2 interpolation-based threshold search. Starting from T0 = pmean with bracket [pmin, pmax], the algorithm evaluates f(T0): since f(T0) > C, val lo is set to T0. Secant 1 connects (val lo, cnt lo) and (val hi, cnt hi), crossing ftarget to determine T1. Since f(T1) < K, val hi is updated to T1, narrowing the bracket. Secant 2 connects the updated anchors and crosses ftarget to produce T2, which lands in the target zone [K, C] in this illustrative example. First-iteration damping (f ≤ 0.50) prevents overshoot.

Table 13: Per-repetition draft acceptance rate (DAR) at OSL=1K for the speculative settings (MTP> 0) under the same fixed-OSL TEP8 setup. The small differences remain within the runto-run variation of speculative decoding.
Table 12: Per-repetition end-to-end TPOT at OSL=1K under the same fixed-OSL TEP8 setup as Figure 11. Reduction is computed from the mean TPOT values across the three alternating A/B repetitions.

Figure 8: Full decode-stage Top-K dispatch. Python level selects between CuTE DSL Top-K (small batch) and the C++ kernel. Within the C++ kernel, the GVR path takes highest priority when preIdx and scratch buffer are available; otherwise the radix-select fallback chain handles the request.

Table 10: Per-phase wall-clock time breakdown of the GVR kernel on B200 (N ≈ 70,690, K = 2,048). Each cell shows absolute time (µs) and percentage of total. Boldface marks the dominant phase per layer.
Table 15: Per-layer distribution characterization from statistical fitting on LongSeqTasks decode logits.

Figure 4: Top: Temporal correlation under an offset+1 shift—when the query advances by one position, all relative positions ∆ shift by +1, causing ∼60% of Top-K tokens to persist (dark navy, shifted right by one cell) while ∼40% change (orange). Bottom-left: Toeplitz structure of the attention score matrix. Bottom-right: Normalized g(∆) comparison—standard RoPE (blue dashed) shows rapid peak decay, while YaRN (dark solid) maintains significant peaks at large ∆, enabling Top-K selection across both nearby and remote positions.

Figure 5: GVR Top-K algorithm flow: Guess (Phases 1–2) estimates a threshold from the previous step’s Top-K, Verify (Phase 3) collects candidates above the threshold, Refine (Phase 4) selects exactly K elements. All four phases execute sequentially within a single CTA. Complexity annotations show per-phase cost; I and S denote the number of threshold-search and snap iterations, respectively.
Table 4: Key kernel optimizations in the GVR Top-K implementation.

Figure 1: Indexer Top-K selection (illustrated with K=5, N=10). The indexer produces a score for each of the N past tokens. The Top-K operator selects the K positions with the highest scores and outputs their indices. In DeepSeek Sparse Attention, K=2048 and N can range from 8K to 128K+.

Figure 10: Per-layer kernel latency (averaged over 17 decode steps) at N ≈ 70,690 on B200. The GVR kernel achieves 1.59×–2.04× speedup vs. the production radix-select baseline across all 9 layers (avg 1.88×). L22 and L41 benefit most (2.04×) due to consistent beta/weibull distributions; L0 benefits least (1.59×) due to heterogeneous lognormal distribution.

Figure 11: End-to-end TPOT reduction at fixed OSL=1K on DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp NVFP4 with TensorRT-LLM on B200×8 under TEP8 parallelism. Colors denote the speculative setting (MTP=0/1/3). GVR gains increase with context length and are largest at MTP=0.

Table 3: Per-phase complexity of the GVR Top-K algorithm. M=2048: prediction set size, N : sequence length, P=512: thread count, I: threshold-search iterations, S: snap iterations, C ≤ 6144: candidate count, Nbin=2048: histogram bins.

Figure 7: Phase 4 detail: 4a scans candidates for min/max range; 4b builds a 2048-bin histogram with warp-parallel K-th bin search (16 warps × 128 bins, 160 serial steps); 4d refines via snap iterations converging when n>(T ) < K ≤ n≥(T ); 4e partitions the final output.

Figure 2: Original decode-stage Top-K dispatch in invokeIndexerTopKDecode (before GVR TopK). The kernel selects insert sort for short sequences, radix sort for medium sequences, and a multi-CTA split approach for very long sequences.
Table 8: Distribution sensitivity: GVR speedup as a function of score distribution type.

Figure 9: Kernel latency vs. sequence length on synthetic data (B200). Both kernels scale as O(N); the GVR kernel’s fitted slope is ∼42% of the baseline, reflecting fewer global-memory passes. Speedup labels show the ratio at representative N values.
Table 14: Memory footprint comparison between radix-select and GVR Top-K.

Table 6: Single-operator latency on B200 with synthetic input. “Production Baseline” is the topKPerRowDecode kernel (insert sort for N < 12288, radix sort for N ≥ 12288). Speedup = baseline time / GVR time.
